article_meta.title: close video GasBuddy expert says claims the US will see 'massive spike' in gas prices are 'erroneous' Patrick De Haan, G

reasoning: The model's response has some issues but is not systematically wrong. It correctly identifies one genuine author-prose bias instance (the 1970s oil shock comparison), has a debatable structural observation about headline/content mismatch, and makes an attribution rule error by flagging 'Zionist regime' which is clearly quoted text from an Iranian source. The model also wraps output in markdown fences despite being told not to.

custom_reasoning.attribution_rule_compliance: The 'Zionist regime' flagging is an attribution rule violation - the model even acknowledges it's a direct quote but flags it anyway. The headline item is debatable since it reflects editorial choices. The 1970s item is correctly from author prose.
custom_reasoning.false_positive_rate: The 'Zionist regime' flag is a false positive since it's quoted text. The headline/content mismatch observation is somewhat defensible as a structural editorial choice but is a stretch. The 1970s comparison is a legitimate flag.
custom_reasoning.false_negative_rate: The model missed some potential bias instances: the embedded link text 'GAS PRICES SURGE, PINCHING AMERICANS AND HANDING THE GOP A NEW MIDTERM HEADACHE' contains editorializing language if it's part of the article's own framing. The phrase 'escalating U.S. and Israeli strikes against the country continue to rattle global energy markets' uses loaded word choices ('rattle'). Also potentially missed slant in the overall framing that heavily emphasizes threat/fear.
custom_reasoning.bias_type_accuracy: The Sensationalism label for the 1970s comparison is reasonable. The Bias by Story Choice and Placement for the headline contradiction is defensible. The Word Choice label for the quoted text is wrong not just because of the type but because it shouldn't have been flagged at all.
